ctx.name: Sam
ctx.notes: Sam is in week three and has started picking up real tickets, but he still hesitates before shipping and asks for reassurance late. I need to understand whether this is a normal confidence dip or a tooling and expectation gap we can fix quickly. I also want clarity on where onboarding handoffs are still fuzzy so he is not quietly compensating. This should stay practical and supportive: map what is unclear, unblock him, and line up the right support touchpoints for the next two weeks.

focusPoints.label: Confidence before shipping and commitment timing.
focusPoints.reason: Sam hesitates before shipping and asks for reassurance late. One possibility is a normal confidence dip in week three; another is a tooling or expectation gap that makes the “ready to ship” bar unclear—worth clarifying which so you can fix it quickly.

focusPoints.label: Onboarding handoffs that still feel fuzzy.
focusPoints.reason: Notes ask for clarity on where onboarding handoffs are still unclear. This could be missing context in ticket intake, unclear ownership boundaries, or dependencies that aren’t explained yet—map the exact handoff points so he’s not compensating quietly.

focusPoints.label: Support touchpoints for the next two weeks.
focusPoints.reason: Whether he’s getting enough lightweight reassurance and fast answers during onboarding is what will determine whether he ramps smoothly or keeps stalling. As a junior in an onboarding check-in, you want to line up specific, time-boxed support moments you can actually deliver.

focusPoints.label: What “done” means for his current tickets.
focusPoints.reason: At junior level, onboarding success often hinges on the concrete definition of “good to ship” (tests, review expectations, edge cases, and who signs off). Making that explicit reduces the need for last-minute reassurance and helps him move independently sooner.

transcript.question: How has week three felt from your side, Sam?
transcript.answer: I can do the tasks, but I still second-guess whether my approach matches team expectations.

transcript.question: When you second-guess your approach, what expectations feel hardest to read?
transcript.answer: Local setup is better now, but I still lose time when the design and API details do not match.

transcript.question: When design and API details do not match, where does it usually break first?
transcript.answer: I am not always sure who should approve what before I push.

transcript.question: Where are the handoffs still fuzzy for you?
transcript.answer: I usually wait too long before asking because I want to solve things myself first.

transcript.question: When you hold back on asking, what are you usually unsure about specifically?
transcript.answer: When feedback is specific and fast, I move much quicker.

transcript.question: feedback specific fast, — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
transcript.answer: I still feel fuzzy on where quality bars differ between quick fixes and core flows.

transcript.question: Who have you found easiest to go to when you get stuck?
transcript.answer: Pairing once or twice a week would probably prevent me from getting stuck silently.

transcript.question: What support from me would help you move faster this fortnight?
transcript.answer: If we define what good looks like for my next two tickets, I will feel much steadier.

transcript.question: If you had to cut your work in half tomorrow, what would you drop first — and what's non-negotiable?
transcript.answer: I want to start by pairing on my next ticket so I can see the approval bar in practice.
